body_txt: A possible result of Artist Disillusionment , some writers, artists, and other creative folk seem to avoid public interviews, interaction with audiences and signing autographs. It seems they prefer to hide behind their work, but in some cases they may even limit how much work they do because it helps them avoid all of the above. Generally fueled by some form of Celebrity is Overrated , specific reasons can vary:  As George Harrison ( maybe ) said: "I wanted to be successful, I never wanted to be famous." So who can blame an artist who tries to disappear from the public eye? At the far end a Reclusive Artist will become notorious for being hard to find ( helpful if they got really wealthy from what they made ahead of time ), reaching into Logical Extremes : Compare and contrast Hikikomori , Hermit Guru , Virtual YouTuber , Celebrity is Overrated . Also compare Persona Non Grata where the reclusiveness is akin to exile. If they only unveil a single masterpiece before dropping off the radar, they're a One-Book Author or One-Hit Wonder . See Eccentric Artist , Sensitive Artist , and Starving Artist for other artist stereotypes.         Comic Strips Webcomics Other   It goes without saying that Hentai manga artists follow this trope, even in very crowded events such as Comiket, Comitia, or COMIC1 . It doesn't help the fact that those events discourage unauthorized photos or videos and they generally enforce a "no photos or videos without permission" policy on those events. Most older manga artists were much more open about appearing in public, especially those who were active in the 1960s and 1970s. Osamu Tezuka , for example, was interviewed many times during his lifetime, and he even made a few public appearances in the United States after Astro Boy had become a huge hit there. Mangaka becoming reclusive mostly came later on.
